key_insight: Piezoelectrically actuated realistic wall deformations can reshape near-wall turbulence, with spanwise waves delivering the strongest drag reduction.
engineering_relevance: The study links finite-element actuator design to DNS-based flow performance, offering a practical pathway for low-drag surfaces in fluid systems.
method: Finite-element analysis of an aluminum sheet driven by piezoelectric actuators
Direct numerical simulation of turbulent half-channel flow at Reτ = 200 with the deformed wall as the boundary condition
Comparison of upstream, downstream, and spanwise wave responses over practical frequency and voltage ranges
evidence_signal: The abstract reports maximum drag reductions of 1.6%, 5.4%, and 27.6% for upstream, downstream, and spanwise waves, respectively, across the simulated actuation range.
watch_item: The results are based on DNS at Reτ = 200 and a specific actuator/sheet setup, so performance may change with geometry, scaling, and real-world manufacturing tolerances.
paper_title: Direct numerical simulations of turbulent drag reduction via piezoelectric actuation
